# Open Acceleration Stack
An open and modular acceleration framework breaking the boundaries between classical and quantum computing: classical acceleration for quantum error correction, calibrations, and more.
[Request a Demo](https://www.quantum-machines.co/contact-us/)
## Overview
Meet Quantum Machines’ Open Acceleration Stack, a modular framework for hybridization of quantum and classical compute resources. It connects QM’s hybrid controller directly with classical accelerators (CPUs, GPUs, and more). The large selection of high-bandwidth integrations and time-bounded communications allows the Open Acceleration Stack to provide the necessary amount of classical compute resources and response latencies needed by your quantum systems. It closes the loop in microseconds, enabling quantum error correction, rapid calibrations, machine-learning routines, adaptive experiments, and paving the way to classically-accelerated quantum supercomputing.

## Powering Quantum Innovation in Leading Labs Worldwide
## An heterogeneous architecture for hybrid quantum-classical supercomputers
The Open Acceleration Stack is a modular framework designed to tightly couple classical compute resources to QPUs with bounded-latency integration, so classical acceleration becomes part of the quantum runtime. At its core is the [OPX1000 Hybrid Controller](https://www.quantum-machines.co/products/opx1000/?utm_source=employee&utm_medium=emp&utm_campaign=ultracoherent_superconducting_cavity-based_multiqudit_platform_with_error-resilient_control), powered by the Pulse Processing Unit. QM’s OPNIC couples the controller to any server, with CPUs, GPUs, FPGAs, and more, from a variety of vendors and any compute power. Boost your quantum system with classical acceleration for real-time calibration of qubit parameters and quantum error correction decoding at scale.
  * NVQLink-compliant, ready for quantum supercomputing.
  * Accelerators-QPU integration with roundtrip latency of 2-4 µs.
  * Supports CUDA, CUDA-Q, C++, Python, & custom device calls.
  * 4-5U rack-mountable hot-swappable system, ready for HPC.


[Request the Open Acceleration Stack Brochure](https://www.quantum-machines.co/request-the-open-acceleration-stack-brochure/)

## Improving quantum workflows: online calibrations with GPU/CPU reinforcement learning
Integrating quantum systems with classical resources is especially valuable for online calibrations, where powerful classical accelerators make it possible to use more computationally demanding methods. With implementations of QM’s Open Acceleration Stack, calibration routines run as a closed loop inside the experiment: the system coordinates shots, streams readout to the classical accelerator, evaluates optimizers or reinforcement-learning (RL) policies, then updates control parameters before the next shot. Connecting an OPX1000 hybrid controller with an NVIDIA GraceHopper, and considering a 3-qubits GHZ routine, we show the online RL optimizes 6 pulse parameters consistently exceeding the best pre-calibrated baseline, and correcting drifts that static routines miss.
The payoff is practical: fewer manual retunes, faster bring-up, higher and steadier fidelities across long runs, and a template that scales to large QPUs and more complex sequences. This demonstrates how tight GPU-CPU-QPU integration transforms calibration from a one-off, time-consuming step into continuous real-time optimization: clear evidence of the dawn of quantum-accelerated supercomputing.
  * 6-parameter tune-up with RL on classical superchip, updated in real time
  * Few-µs runtimes, maximizing correction bandwidth and QPU utilization
  * Consistently superior fidelity and stability compared to baseline


[Learn more in GHZ](https://www.quantum-machines.co/seminars/realtime-classical-quantum-computations-when-your-qubits-are-fast/)
[Learn more in GHZ](https://www.quantum-machines.co/seminars/realtime-classical-quantum-computations-when-your-qubits-are-fast/)
## Integrating classical resources for quantum error correction decoding at scale
Quantum error correction only works if the decoding speed keeps up with the error rate, and as QPUs scale up, the required classical computation becomes increasingly resource-intensive. QM’s Open Acceleration Stack allows QEC decoding to run on high-performance classical accelerators, so the heavy lifting happens on a powerful CPU-GPU server. The quantum system controller runs stabilizer rounds on the QPU, and streams error syndromes (measurement results) to the server. A separate kernel (e.g. CUDA kernel) decodes syndromes and computes corrections, sending the information back to the controller, where feed-forward operations close the loop. Between an OPX1000 and an NVIDIA GraceHopper, the communication roundtrip latency is kept below 4 µs, allowing the decoding time budget to remain below the 10-20 µs limit for useful decoding [Kurman, et al. arXiv:2412.00289 (2024)]. The result of using the Open Acceleration Stack to integrate acceleration servers into quantum sequences is real-time QEC that scales: larger codes, deeper rounds, and higher-throughput experiments without rewriting the stack.
Protocols can be written in CUDA-Q: a single, unified hybrid programming language that defines a quantum circuit, data streaming, device calls, and the quantum feedback to apply corrections. This keeps real-time quantum orchestration, decoding, and control in one place, aligned and fully compatible with NVIDIA NVQLink’s open model for bounded-latency quantum-classical integration. As codes grow and decoders evolve, classically accelerated QEC decoding offers the scale and flexibility needed for fault-tolerant quantum computing.

## Key Features
The first system to integrate classical accelerators in quantum sequences, and demonstrate QEC workflows and AI-based calibrations on GPU.
Microsecond-level controller-accelerator roundtrip latencies (2-4 μs).
Natively supports programming in CUDA, CUDA-Q, C++, Python, and custom device calls.
Designed for any scale, from cost-efficient servers to cutting-edge data centers.
## Benefits
Run the Most Advanced Hybrid Algorithms
The only solution with demonstrated support for quantum error correction and reinforcement-learning-based calibrations
Effortless Scalability
Add OPX1000 controllers as your QPU grows, and add accelerator servers to meet increasing computational demands
HPC & Data Center Ready
HPC-QC integration from the hardware quantum control layer and up, designed for data center reliability with redundancies and hot swappable critical parts
Compatible with All Qubit Technologies
The Open Acceleration Stack supports superconducting qubits, spin qubits, neutral atoms, trapped ions, defect centers, photonic qubits and more
Wide Language Support
The Open Acceleration Stack natively supports programming in CUDA, CUDA-Q, C++, Python, and custom device calls, for co-development of hybrid workflows.

}

}

}

}

  display: inline-flex;
  align-items: center;
}

@media (prefers-color-scheme: dark) /* explorer */ {
  mjx-help > svg {
    stroke: #E0E0E0;
  }
  mjx-help > svg > circle {
    fill: #404040;
  }
  mjx-help > svg > circle:nth-child(2) {
    fill: rgba(132, 132, 255, .3);
  }
  mjx-help:hover > svg > circle:nth-child(2) {
    stroke: #AAAAAA;
      fill: #404040;
  }
}

                line-height: 140%;
                color: #999999;
            }
        }

        .fixed-element {
            position: absolute;
            z-index: 10;
            transition: opacity 0.3s ease;
            width: 100%;
            pointer-events: none;
        }

        .fixed-element.fixed-text {
            font-weight: 400;
            color: #fff;
            text-align: center;
            pointer-events: none;

        .fixed-element:has(.prod-info-wrapp.opened) {
            z-index: 50!important;

        .fixed-element img {
            height: auto;
            pointer-events: auto;
        }

        /* Connection lines */
        .connections {
            position: absolute;
            top: 0;
            left: 0;
            width: 100%;
            height: 100%;
            pointer-events: none;
            z-index: 1;
        }

        .connection-line {
            stroke: #fff;
            stroke-width: 0.23;
            fill: none;
            stroke-dasharray: 0.2 0.6;
            animation: flow 5.5s linear infinite;
        }

        /* Compensate for 9:16 aspect ratio stretching on horizontal lines */
        .line-fpga, .line-qeci, .line-gpu, .line-NVIDIA, .line-cpu, .line-riverlane, .line-opx, .line-main-horizontal-NVIDIA, .line-main-vertical-CPU {
            stroke-width: 0.17; /* Thinner to compensate for vertical stretching */
            stroke-dasharray: 0.35 1.1; /* Larger dashes to compensate for horizontal compression */
        }

        /* Hide partial vertical lines by default (Full mode) */
        .line-main-vertical-CPU,
        .line-NVIDIA,
        .line-main-vertical-RL {
            opacity: 0;
        }

        .connection-line.golden {
            stroke: #fff;
            stroke-width: 0.35; /* Thinner to compensate for vertical stretching */
            stroke-dasharray: 0.2 0.6;
            animation: flow-golden 0.8s linear infinite;
        }

        .connection-line.red {
            stroke: #fff;
            stroke-width: 0.5; /* Thinner to compensate for vertical stretching */
            stroke-dasharray: 0.2 0.8;
            animation: flow 7s linear infinite;
        }

        @keyframes flow {
            0% {
                stroke-dashoffset: 0;
            }
            50% {
                stroke-dashoffset: -15;
            }
            100% {
                stroke-dashoffset: 0;
            }
        }

        @keyframes flow-golden {
            0% {
                stroke-dashoffset: 0;
            }
            50% {
                stroke-dashoffset: 3;
            }
            100% {
                stroke-dashoffset: 0;
            }
        }

        .mode-indicator {
            position: absolute;
            bottom: 0;
            left: 50%;
            transform: translateX(-50%);
            background: none;
            border: none;
            border-radius: 1vh;
            padding: 0.8vh 1.5vh;
            z-index: 100;
            box-shadow: none;
            display: flex;
            align-items: center;
            gap: 3vh;
            white-space: nowrap;
        }
        .mode-indicator input[type="checkbox"]:checked {
            background: #009EDE;
        }

        .mode-indicator input[type="checkbox"]:checked::after {
            content: '✓';
            position: absolute;
            top: 50%;
            left: 50%;
            transform: translate(-50%, -50%);
            font-size: 0.8vh;
            color: #1a1a1a;
            font-weight: bold;
        }
        .clickable-image {
            cursor: pointer;
            transition: opacity 0.2s;
        }

        .clickable-image:hover {
            opacity: 0.8;
        }

        .rl-box {
            position: absolute;
            left: 55.5%;
            top: 36%;
            width: 29%;
            height: 4%;
            border: 0.3vh solid rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.13);
            border-radius: 1vh;
            background: transparent;
            z-index: 14;
            pointer-events: none;
        }

        /* Hide elements in decoder mode */
        .decoder-mode #Intel1,
        .decoder-mode #Intel2,
        .decoder-mode #AMD1,
        .decoder-mode #AMD2,
        .decoder-mode #NVIDIA,
        .decoder-mode #L40s,

        .decoder-mode #text_L40s,

        /* Hide blue lines in decoder mode */
        .decoder-mode .line-gpu,

        .decoder-mode .line-fpga,
        .decoder-mode .line-NVIDIA,
            /* .decoder-mode .line-qeci, */
        .decoder-mode .line-main-vertical,
        .decoder-mode .line-main-horizontal-NVIDIA,
        .decoder-mode .line-qeci,
            /* .decoder-mode .line-opx, */
        .decoder-mode .line-main-vertical-CPU {
            opacity: 0;
        }

        .decoder-mode #text_RL,
        .decoder-mode #text_opnicQECi,
        .decoder-mode .line-main-vertical-RL {
            opacity: 1;
        }

        /* Hide elements in GPU and CPU mode */

        .gpu-cpu-mode .line-main-horizontal-NVIDIA,
        .gpu-cpu-mode .line-main-vertical-RL {
            opacity: 0;
        }

        .gpu-cpu-mode .line-main-vertical {
            opacity: 1;
        }

        /* Hide elements in NVIDIA (NVlink) mode */
        .nvlink-mode #RL,
        .nvlink-mode #rl-box,

        .nvlink-mode #L40s,

        .nvlink-mode .line-qeci,

        .nvlink-mode .line-main-horizontal-NVIDIA,

        }

        /* Hide elements in FPGA mode */

        OPX1000
      </div>

        HPC
      </div>
      <div class="fixed-element fixed-text hmfs" id="text_RL" style="left: 54%; top: 34%; width: 32%;">
        EXTERNAL DECODER
      </div>

      <!-- Mode Indicator -->
        <div class="mode-indicator">
            <h3 style="color: #fff; font-size: 1.1vh; margin: 0; text-transform: uppercase; letter-spacing: 0.05vh;">Mode:</h3>
            <div id="currentMode" style="font-size: 1.1vh; color: #009EDE; font-weight: bold;">NVidia AArch64 NVqlink</div>
            <label style="display: flex; align-items: center; margin-bottom: 0; cursor: pointer; font-size: 1.1vh; color: #fff;">
                <input type="checkbox" id="autoMode" checked="" style="appearance: none; margin-top: 0; -webkit-appearance: none; width: 1.2vh; height: 1.2vh; border: 0.15vh solid #009EDE; border-radius: 0.2vh; margin-right: 0.5vh; cursor: pointer; position: relative; transition: all 0.2s;">
                Cycle
            </label>
        </div>

    </div>

    <script>

        const CYCLE_DURATION = 6000;  // Time between mode changes in milliseconds (3 seconds)
        const RETURN_TO_CYCLE_TIMEOUT = 30000;  // Time before returning to auto-cycle after manual selection (30 seconds)

        let autoInterval = null;
        let returnToCycleTimeout = null;
        const modes = ['full', 'nvlink', 'gpu-cpu', 'fpga', 'decoder'];
        let currentModeIndex = 0;
        const modeNames = {
            'full': 'Full server configuration',
            'nvlink': 'NVidia AArch64 NVqlink',
            'gpu-cpu': 'CPU x64/x86\nand/or Nvidia GPU',
            'fpga': 'FPGA Decoders',
            'decoder': 'External Decoder - Riverlane'
        };

        function updateModeDisplay(mode) {
            document.getElementById('currentMode').textContent = modeNames[mode];
        }

        function setMode(mode) {
            const container = document.querySelector('.server-container');

            // Remove all mode classes
            container.classList.remove('decoder-mode', 'fpga-mode', 'gpu-cpu-mode', 'nvlink-mode', 'full');

            // Add the selected mode class
            if (mode === 'decoder') {
                container.classList.add('decoder-mode');
            } else if (mode === 'fpga') {
                container.classList.add('fpga-mode');
            } else if (mode === 'gpu-cpu') {
                container.classList.add('gpu-cpu-mode');
            } else if (mode === 'nvlink') {
                container.classList.add('nvlink-mode');
            } else {
                container.classList.add('full');
            }

            // Update mode display
            updateModeDisplay(mode);
        }

        function cycleMode() {
            currentModeIndex = (currentModeIndex + 1) % modes.length;
            setMode(modes[currentModeIndex]);
        }

        function startCycleMode() {
            document.getElementById('autoMode').checked = true;
            cycleMode(); // Start immediately
            autoInterval = setInterval(cycleMode, CYCLE_DURATION);
        }

        function stopCycleMode() {
            document.getElementById('autoMode').checked = false;
            if (autoInterval) {
                clearInterval(autoInterval);
                autoInterval = null;
            }
        }

        function clearReturnToCycleTimer() {
            if (returnToCycleTimeout) {
                clearTimeout(returnToCycleTimeout);
                returnToCycleTimeout = null;
            }
        }

        function scheduleReturnToCycle() {
            clearReturnToCycleTimer();
            returnToCycleTimeout = setTimeout(() => {
                startCycleMode();
                returnToCycleTimeout = null;
            }, RETURN_TO_CYCLE_TIMEOUT);
        }

        // Handle image clicks
        document.querySelectorAll('.clickable-image').forEach(img => {
            img.addEventListener('click', (e) => {
                const modeElement = e.target.closest('[data-mode]');
                if (!modeElement) {
                    console.error('No data-mode element found for:', e.target.src);
                    return;
                }
                const mode = modeElement.getAttribute('data-mode');
                stopCycleMode();
                currentModeIndex = modes.indexOf(mode);
                setMode(mode);
                scheduleReturnToCycle();
            });
        });

        // Handle auto checkbox
        document.getElementById('autoMode').addEventListener('change', (e) => {
            if (e.target.checked) {
                clearReturnToCycleTimer(); // Cancel any pending return to cycle
                startCycleMode();
            } else {
                stopCycleMode();
                // Don't schedule return to cycle when manually unchecked
            }
        });

        const wrappers = document.querySelectorAll('.prod-info-wrapp');

        wrappers.forEach(wrapper => {
            const trigger = wrapper.querySelector('.prod-info-trigger');
            const closeBtn = wrapper.querySelector('.prod-info-close');

            trigger.addEventListener('click', function (e) {
                e.stopPropagation();
                wrapper.classList.toggle('opened');
            });

            closeBtn.addEventListener('click', function () {
                wrapper.classList.remove('opened');
            });
        });

        document.addEventListener('click', function (e) {
            wrappers.forEach(wrapper => {
                if (!wrapper.contains(e.target)) {
                    wrapper.classList.remove('opened');
                }
            });
        });

        // Start auto mode on page load
        startCycleMode();
    </script>
</section>

																			<section data-wpr-lazyrender="1" class="hardware-wrapper background-light-blue title-h2-as-h3 section-padding relative  after-bg-image-wrapper dark-layout background-black											grey-layout background-grey											" data-layout="home_section_six">

														<div class="flex-6 box box-text ani opacity">
															<div class="text"><h2>An heterogeneous architecture for hybrid quantum-classical supercomputers</h2>
<p>The Open Acceleration Stack is a modular framework designed to tightly couple classical compute resources to QPUs with bounded-latency integration, so classical acceleration becomes part of the quantum runtime. At its core is the <a href="https://www.quantum-machines.co/products/opx1000/?utm_source=employee&utm_medium=emp&utm_campaign=ultracoherent_superconducting_cavity-based_multiqudit_platform_with_error-resilient_control" target="_blank" rel="noopener">OPX1000 Hybrid Controller</a>, powered by the Pulse Processing Unit. QM’s OPNIC couples the controller to any server, with CPUs, GPUs, FPGAs, and more, from a variety of vendors and any compute power. Boost your quantum system with classical acceleration for real-time calibration of qubit parameters and quantum error correction decoding at scale.</p>
<ul>
<li>NVQLink-compliant, ready for quantum supercomputing.</li>
<li>Accelerators-QPU integration with roundtrip latency of 2-4 µs.</li>
<li><span>Supports CUDA, CUDA-Q, C++, Python, & custom device calls.</span></li>
<li>4-5U rack-mountable hot-swappable system, ready for HPC.</li>
</ul>
																													<div class="button-wrap desktop">
																		<a data-event-name="call_to_action" class="button background-blue border-blue font-white" href="https://www.quantum-machines.co/request-the-open-acceleration-stack-brochure/" target="_blank">Request the Open Acceleration Stack Brochure</a>
																	</div>
																										</div>
														</div>
														<div class="flex-6 box box-image left ani opacity ani-delay ">

																</div>
																																				<div class="button-wrap mobile">
																	<a data-event-name="call_to_action" class="button background-blue border-blue font-white" href="https://www.quantum-machines.co/request-the-open-acceleration-stack-brochure/" target="_blank">Request the Brochure</a>

																													<div id="" class="flex-row row align-items-center even in-mobile-reverse-order ">
														<div class="flex-6 box box-text ani opacity">
															<div class="text"><h2>Improving quantum workflows: online calibrations with GPU/CPU reinforcement learning</h2>
<p>Integrating quantum systems with classical resources is especially valuable for online calibrations, where powerful classical accelerators make it possible to use more computationally demanding methods. With implementations of QM’s Open Acceleration Stack, calibration routines run as a closed loop inside the experiment: the system coordinates shots, streams readout to the classical accelerator, evaluates optimizers or reinforcement-learning (RL) policies, then updates control parameters before the next shot. Connecting an OPX1000 hybrid controller with an NVIDIA GraceHopper, and considering a 3-qubits GHZ routine, we show the online RL optimizes 6 pulse parameters consistently exceeding the best pre-calibrated baseline, and correcting drifts that static routines miss.</p>
<p>The payoff is practical: fewer manual retunes, faster bring-up, higher and steadier fidelities across long runs, and a template that scales to large QPUs and more complex sequences. This demonstrates how tight GPU-CPU-QPU integration transforms calibration from a one-off, time-consuming step into continuous real-time optimization: clear evidence of the dawn of quantum-accelerated supercomputing.</p>
<ul>
<li>6-parameter tune-up with RL on classical superchip, updated in real time</li>
<li>Few-µs runtimes, maximizing correction bandwidth and QPU utilization</li>
<li>Consistently superior fidelity and stability compared to baseline</li>
</ul>
																													<div class="button-wrap desktop">
																		<a data-event-name="call_to_action" class="button background-blue border-blue font-white" href="https://www.quantum-machines.co/seminars/realtime-classical-quantum-computations-when-your-qubits-are-fast/" target="_blank">Learn more in GHZ</a>
																	</div>
																										</div>
														</div>
														<div class="flex-6 box box-image left ani opacity ani-delay ">
																											<div class="image  " style="width:100%">

																	<a data-event-name="call_to_action" class="button background-blue border-blue font-white" href="https://www.quantum-machines.co/seminars/realtime-classical-quantum-computations-when-your-qubits-are-fast/" target="_blank">Learn more in GHZ</a>

																													<div id="" class="flex-row row align-items-center odd in-mobile-reverse-order ">
														<div class="flex-6 box box-text ani opacity">
															<div class="text"><h2>Integrating classical resources for quantum error correction decoding at scale</h2>
<p>Quantum error correction only works if the decoding speed keeps up with the error rate, and as QPUs scale up, the required classical computation becomes increasingly resource-intensive. QM’s Open Acceleration Stack allows QEC decoding to run on high-performance classical accelerators, so the heavy lifting happens on a powerful CPU-GPU server. The quantum system controller runs stabilizer rounds on the QPU, and streams error syndromes (measurement results) to the server. A separate kernel (e.g. CUDA kernel) decodes syndromes and computes corrections, sending the information back to the controller, where feed-forward operations close the loop. Between an OPX1000 and an NVIDIA GraceHopper, the communication roundtrip latency is kept below 4 µs, allowing the decoding time budget to remain below the 10-20 µs limit for useful decoding [Kurman, et al. arXiv:2412.00289 (2024)]. The result of using the Open Acceleration Stack to integrate acceleration servers into quantum sequences is real-time QEC that scales: larger codes, deeper rounds, and higher-throughput experiments without rewriting the stack.</p>
<p>Protocols can be written in CUDA-Q: a single, unified hybrid programming language that defines a quantum circuit, data streaming, device calls, and the quantum feedback to apply corrections. This keeps real-time quantum orchestration, decoding, and control in one place, aligned and fully compatible with NVIDIA NVQLink’s open model for bounded-latency quantum-classical integration. As codes grow and decoders evolve, classically accelerated QEC decoding offers the scale and flexibility needed for fault-tolerant quantum computing.</p>

/* support for background images: */
document.addEventListener('lazybeforeunveil', function (e) {var bg = e.target.getAttribute('data-bg');if (bg) e.target.style.backgroundImage = 'url(' + bg + ')';});

/* load new background-image */
function loadImg(element){
    var bg = '';
	switch (screenW) {
		case  'mobile':
			bg = element.getAttribute('data-bg-mobile');
			if(bg)
				break; 
		case 'tablet':
			bg = element.getAttribute('data-bg-tablet');
			if(bg)
				break; 
		case 'laptop':
			bg = element.getAttribute('data-bg-laptop');
			if(bg)
				break; 
		default: 
			bg = element.getAttribute('data-bg');
	}
	if(bg)
		element.style.backgroundImage = 'url(' + bg + ')';	
}

/* calc screen wide */
var screenW;
var first = true;
jQuery(window).on('resize', function(){
	calcScreenW();
});
function calcScreenW(){
	var screenC = false;
	curentW= jQuery(window).width();	
	if ( curentW > 1450 ) { 
		if (screenW != 'desktop'){screenW = 'desktop';screenC = true;	}
	} else if (curentW > 973){
		if (screenW != 'laptop'){screenW = 'laptop';screenC = true;}		
	} else if (curentW >767){
		if (screenW != 'tablet'){screenW = 'tablet';	screenC = true;}		
	} else {
		if (screenW != 'mobile'){screenW = 'mobile'; screenC = true;}		
	}
	if (screenC) {
		jQuery('.bg-change-done').removeClass('bg-change-done');
		screenC = false;
		animate_elems_bg();
	}
}
jQuery(function () {
	calcScreenW();
});

/* lazyLoad on scroll after  screen width change */
var $elems_bg = jQuery('.responsive-bg');
var winheight = jQuery(window).height();
jQuery(window).scroll(function(){	animate_elems_bg();});
function animate_elems_bg() {
	wintop = jQuery(window).scrollTop(); 
	$elems_bg.each(function(){
		$elm_bg = jQuery(this);
		if($elm_bg.hasClass('lazyload')|| $elm_bg.hasClass('bg-change-done')) { return true; } 
		topcoords = $elm_bg.offset().top; 
		if(wintop > (topcoords - (winheight))) {			
			$elm_bg.addClass('bg-change-done');		
			loadImg($elm_bg[0]);
		} 
	});	
}

</script>
